a282c4282fca.source: Yes, every triangle follows the rule "area = 1/2 base * height". But the relationship between base and height depends on the type of triangle (base = 2 * height, base = 3 * height, etc.), so even then the area factor will be different.
a282c4282fca.target: 是的，每个三角形都遵循“面积 = 1/2 * 底 * 高”的规则。但底和高之间的关系取决于三角形的类型（底 = 2 * 高，底 = 3 * 高等），所以即使如此，面积因子也会不同。

8b1f45ca039c.source: Why do we need similar shapes to keep the same area equation? Intuitively, when you zoom (scale) a shape, you're changing the absolute size but not the relative ratios within the shape. A square, no matter how zoomed, has a perimeter = 4 * side.
8b1f45ca039c.target: 为什么我们需要相似形状才能保持相同的面积方程？直观地说，当缩放一个形状时，改变的是绝对大小，而不是形状内部的相对比例。正方形无论怎么缩放，其周长都等于 4 * 边长。

771d324d2381.source: Pretty interesting. 70 elements spread among two groups can be sorted as fast as 50 items in one group. (Yeah, there may be constant overhead/start up time, just work with me here).
771d324d2381.target: 相当有趣。分布在两个组中的70个元素可以与一个组中的50个元素一样快地排序。(是的,可能有恒定的开销/启动时间,先凑合着看吧)。

46bb2be6d204.source: Given this relationship, it makes sense to partition elements into separate groups and then sort the subgroups. Indeed, that's the approach used in quicksort, one of the best general-purpose sorting methods. The Pythagorean theorem helps show how sorting 50 combined elements can be as slow as sorting 30 and 40 separate ones.
46bb2be6d204.target: 鉴于这种关系,将元素划分到不同的组中然后对子组进行排序是有意义的。事实上,这是快速排序(最好的通用排序方法之一)所采用的方法。勾股定理有助于说明为什么对50个组合元素进行排序可能和对30和40个单独元素进行排序一样慢。

24ac70a038e8.source: . We all have different thoughts and I’m throwing my own into the ring.
24ac70a038e8.target: 。我们都有不同的想法，我把自己的也摆出来。

5f9a56de9177.source: Most articles on career happiness spout abstract quotes about following your passions. Sure, those thoughts are nice (and I’ll link to a few), but I want to share the
5f9a56de9177.target: 大多数关于职业幸福感的文章都充斥着关于追随激情的抽象引语。当然，这些想法很好（我会链接一些），但我想分享

a46371a7d018.source: And above all: Don’t trick yourself.
a46371a7d018.target: 最重要的是：不要欺骗自己。

bc597d71f783.source: Ah, the tough question which started your quest and the one you may dread:
bc597d71f783.target: 啊，那个开启你探索之旅的难题，也是你可能害怕的问题：

4c9be27983f6.source: What are you going to do? Isn’t the regular life good enough?
4c9be27983f6.target: 你打算做什么？普通的生活难道不够好吗？

8bb9e4d1e65c.source: There’s no easy answer. Even months after
8bb9e4d1e65c.target: 没有简单的答案。即使在几个月之后

c652bb3a18b7.source: , the decision isn’t crystal clear. Only now, after looking backwards for half a year, am I comfortable with my choice.
c652bb3a18b7.target: ，决定并不明确。只有在回望半年后的现在，我才对自己的选择感到满意。

fc628d1bc557.source: Thinking about it more, I don’t

6fc6f10ac338.source: there to be a solid answer to “What should I do?”. Imagine being told your life’s work at age 12: “You’ll be a Molecular Biologist”. Sure, that job may be great for you, but wouldn’t you at least wonder about the other options?
6fc6f10ac338.target: 认为“我该怎么做？”有一个确定的答案。想象一下在12岁时被告知你一生的事业：“你将成为一名分子生物学家。”当然，那份工作可能很适合你，但难道你至少不会好奇其他选择吗？

854ebe5c33b4.source: I want life to be more of a surprise, though I realize not everyone is like this. I think it’s ok to be lost, fight through some tough times which inspire you to search for what you enjoy.
854ebe5c33b4.target: 我希望生活充满更多惊喜，尽管我意识到并非每个人都这样。我认为迷茫是可以接受的，经历一些艰难时期会激励你寻找自己真正喜欢的东西。

924d99904b4c.source: You need rain to appreciate sunshine — and both are needed to make the beautiful, brilliant, one-in-a-million flower of your heart grow. Said another way, you need to touch a hot stove to
924d99904b4c.target: 你需要雨才能欣赏阳光——而两者都是培育你心中那朵美丽、灿烂、独一无二的花朵所必需的。换句话说，你需要摸一下热炉子才能

575ca234251c.source: that hot stoves aren’t for you. Take your pick of analogy, the “I’m a special snowflake” stuff isn’t really my style.
575ca234251c.target: 明白热炉子不适合你。随便选个比喻吧，“我是特别的小雪花”这种说法其实不是我的风格。

0d5affaaa124.source: when thinking about your life. Many people justify past decisions because they want them to make sense and feel better.  “Yes, I like wandering around and then finding my passion because it makes me appreciate it more,” you might have heard someone say. For some, it seems that no matter what happens, it was the “right thing” or that it was “meant to be that way”. I’m on the fence about whether this is a coping mechanism, optimistic viewpoint (I’m an optimist), or whether it makes you appreciate life more.
0d5affaaa124.target: 陷阱。许多人合理化过去的决定，因为他们希望这些决定有意义，让自己感觉更好。“是的，我喜欢四处游荡然后找到激情，因为这让我更珍惜它，”你可能听过有人这么说。对某些人来说，似乎无论发生什么，都是“正确的事”或“本该如此”。我对此持保留态度，不确定这是一种应对机制、乐观的观点（我是个乐观主义者），还是能让你更珍惜生活。

dd1258c4ca27.source: Yes, most clouds have silver linings. But sometimes getting hit in the head with a shovel is
dd1258c4ca27.target: 是的，大多数乌云都有银边。但有时候被铲子打中脑袋就是

199abf7d93d8.source: . It’s not fun, there’s not much educational value, and your life really is better without it.
199abf7d93d8.target: 。这不好玩，没什么教育意义，没有它你的生活真的会更好。

b0b6be1378ea.source: . They see an effect and start looking frantically for its cause. While I believe in cause and effect, I don’t always believe in mankind’s ability to interpret them. Do EMTs cause heart attacks because cities with more EMTs have more emergency calls? Surely not, but most arguments aren’t as easy to disentangle. Many people love to dispense advice and pretend they know
b0b6be1378ea.target: 。他们看到结果就开始疯狂寻找原因。虽然我相信因果关系，但我并不总是相信人类解读它们的能力。难道急救人员会导致心脏病发作吗？因为急救人员越多的城市紧急呼叫也越多？当然不是，但大多数争论并不那么容易理清。许多人喜欢给出建议，假装知道事情

0185bb64a203.source: things happened the way they did. I tend to be more skeptical of this.
0185bb64a203.target: 为什么会这样发生。我对此往往更加怀疑。

426a6ccbae1e.source: I’m aware of but not immune to these faults (neither are you, most likely), so take this with a grain of salt. Take each person’s experience as just that:
426a6ccbae1e.target: 我意识到这些缺点但并非免疫（你也很可能如此），所以请对此持保留态度。把每个人的经历仅仅当作：

898f36f959d7.source: Also recognize that we aren’t always perfect snowflakes all the time. Have the courage to admit where you need to improve. Now let’s get onto the tips.
898f36f959d7.target: 也要认识到我们并不总是完美的小雪花。要有勇气承认需要改进的地方。现在让我们进入建议部分。

17051dbb8e45.source: I don’t remember where I read this, but I love the idea. Keep a simple text file on your computer, mine’s called feelgood.txt. Whenever you have an experience that really makes you happy, put in an entry, even just a few words or a sentence. Here are some of mine:
17051dbb8e45.target: 我不记得在哪里读到过这个，但我很喜欢这个想法。在你的电脑上保存一个简单的文本文件，我的叫feelgood.txt。每当你有一段真正让你开心的经历，就记一笔，哪怕只是几个字或一句话。以下是我的一些记录：

d45c968eb1d6.source: Some days you’ll have five. Sometimes I’ll go a week or more without writing in it. The goal is to start keeping track of what makes you get excited about life.
d45c968eb1d6.target: 有些日子你会写五条。有时我可能会一周或更久不写。目标是开始记录那些让你对生活感到兴奋的事情。

43f918470e9e.source: Here’s the rub: if you try and sit down and name your favorite things, you might think of a few off the top of your head. But this is in the moment. You’ve forgotten about what made you happy last week, last month or last year.
43f918470e9e.target: 问题在于：如果你试着坐下来列举自己喜欢的事情，你可能会随口说出几个。但这只是当下的想法。你已经忘记了上周、上个月或去年让你快乐的事情。

290971f4e48d.source: The feelgood list lets you look back and

cb9d138376ff.source: in what you like. Here are a few patterns I noticed:
cb9d138376ff.target: 你所喜欢的东西。以下是我注意到的一些模式：

47c433f53b57.source: These trends seem “obvious” to me in hindsight… but how do you
47c433f53b57.target: 事后看来，这些趋势似乎“显而易见”……但你如何

83fe32f10e6a.source: without looking at your past? Our memories aren’t perfect and it’s hard to see trends in your own experience.
83fe32f10e6a.target: 在不回顾过去的情况下发现它们呢？我们的记忆并不完美，而且很难从自己的经历中看出趋势。

5f20974b4a2c.source: The feelgood list is also a great way to pick yourself up when feeling down: reading the old entries recreates the feeling I had at the time. Sometimes a “minor” word of encouragement (from the giver’s perspective) can have a huge effect on you. One small compliment may really stick with you and encourage you try something new or keep going (like Ron’s comment while snowboarding — he likely doesn’t remember).
5f20974b4a2c.target: 愉快清单也是一种在情绪低落时振作起来的好方法：阅读过去的记录能重现我当时的感觉。有时一句（从给予者角度看）‘微不足道’的鼓励话语也能对你产生巨大影响。一句小小的赞美可能会真正伴随你，鼓励你尝试新事物或继续前进（比如罗恩在滑雪时的评论——他可能不记得了）。

34ebd4998c2b.source: This can work both ways — an offhand, snide remark can turn you off from a subject forever, so watch out for this. It’s hard to predict what has an impact on you — just write down what does.
34ebd4998c2b.target: 这也可以是双向的——一句随意的、讽刺的评论可能会让你永远对某个话题失去兴趣，所以要注意这一点。很难预测什么会对你有影响——只需记下那些确实有影响的。

88f815fc2b7d.source: Keep a feelgood list. Review it peridiocally. Repeat.
88f815fc2b7d.target: 保持一份愉快清单。定期回顾。重复。

f00209147a01.source: More lists! Keep another file (ideas.txt, .doc, .xls, .etc) to write down your awesome ideas.
f00209147a01.target: 更多清单！保留另一个文件（ideas.txt, .doc, .xls等）来记录你绝妙的想法。

da960f5c6c2d.source: “Uh… what ideas?” you’re probably thinking. You have them, probably a dozen a day. The problem, again, is if I ask you for your ideas you’ll give me a list of 5, the things you thought about just now. That’s not good enough, chump. I want dozens, hundreds.
da960f5c6c2d.target: “呃……什么想法？”你可能会想。你其实有很多想法，大概一天十几个。问题还是，如果我问你有什么想法，你会列出5个，就是刚才想到的那些。那可不够，伙计。我想要几十个、几百个。

c54736f4252e.source: If you’re like me (all I know is my own experience), your head is constantly buzzing with thought. You’re in line at the grocery store — you’re thinking of stuff. You’re driving — you’re thinking of stuff. You’re “zoning out”, or so it appears — you’re actually thinking of stuff.
c54736f4252e.target: 如果你像我一样（我只了解自己的经历），你的头脑中会不断有想法在嗡嗡作响。你在杂货店排队——你在想事情。你在开车——你在想事情。你‘走神’了，或者看起来是这样——你实际上在想事情。

66e19279007f.source: You’re doing all this thought, some about new ideas, and nothing is saved. No “Untitled Document (1)” is sitting around in your brain. And that’s a problem.
66e19279007f.target: 你进行了所有这些思考，有些是关于新想法的，但什么也没保存。你的大脑里并没有一个‘未命名文档（1）’在闲置。这就是问题所在。

04827bd2cca3.source: InstaCalc, Better Explained, and a few unreleased projects are the result of these idea lists. Sure, not every idea is a winner — but you need to have some in order to pick and choose. Out of 50+ ideas in my list, perhaps 5 or 6 are “winners” that I feel really excited about doing.
04827bd2cca3.target: InstaCalc、Better Explained 以及一些未发布的项目都是这些想法清单的产物。当然，并非每个想法都是赢家——但你需要有一些才能择优选取。在我列表中的50多个想法里，大概有5、6个是我真正感到兴奋并想去做的‘胜者’。

5c27923f8347.source: And speaking of winners, remember that

cfa7b9891b32.source: . You are capturing version 1 of the thought. If it’s interesting to you, you’ll come back a week later and make version 1.5. Then version 2, then version 10. InstaCalc is probably on version 500 in terms of the number of enhancements and features I’ve added since when I’ve started. And “a faster online calculator” doesn’t sound that sexy as a V1 idea, but it can still become something useful with some iteration.
cfa7b9891b32.target: 你捕捉的是想法的版本1。如果你觉得它有趣，一周后你会回来做出版本1.5。然后是版本2，再然后是版本10。从我开始以来，InstaCalc 的增强和功能可能已经到了版本500。而‘一个更快的在线计算器’作为V1想法听起来并不那么性感，但经过一些迭代，它仍然可以成为有用的东西。

a6983c796576.source: You can’t get to version 500 if you don’t start with a version 1.
a6983c796576.target: 如果你不从版本1开始，就无法达到版本500。

3175ec4c9110.source: Your ideas are another insight into your mind — the things you think about, enjoy, and want to make better. Most of my ideas are around services that I’d find useful as a programmer or learner, and enhancements to existing tools. They are mostly software-related because that’s my background, though some are around education and communication.
3175ec4c9110.target: 你的想法是对你内心的另一种洞察——那些你思考、享受并希望改进的事物。我的大部分想法都是关于作为程序员或学习者我觉得有用的服务，以及对现有工具的增强。它们大多与软件相关，因为那是我的背景，尽管有些涉及教育和沟通。

22dad931f8fc.source: The best part about keeping a list is that your ideas multiply like rabbits in your notebook. Each idea new idea can be combined with each of the old ones, leading to even more variations. Next week when you have yet another idea, it can enhance the older ones and the cycle keeps going.
22dad931f8fc.target: 保持清单最好的部分是，你的想法像兔子在笔记本里一样繁殖。每个新想法都可以与每个旧想法结合，产生更多的变体。下周当你又有新想法时，它可以增强旧的想法，循环不断。

a586900ed8aa.source: I also keep a stack of index cards by my bed — I sometimes have brainstorms before going to sleep. I can remember nights where I’d be lying on my pillow and then
a586900ed8aa.target: 我也在床边放了一叠索引卡——我有时在睡觉前会有头脑风暴。我记得那些夜晚，我躺在枕头上，然后

77586ce517c4.source: , I’d get up a dozen times to write down a thought. It was a bit annoying at the time (“I just want to sleep!!!”) but my brain kept firing. I’m thankful now — most of those thoughts turned into features for InstaCalc.
77586ce517c4.target: 我可能会起身十几次记下一个想法。当时有点烦人（“我只想睡觉！！！”），但我的大脑一直在活跃。现在我很感激——这些想法大多成为了InstaCalc的功能。

dd761683d7be.source: (for offline use) to track those ideas streaming from your unique snowflake head. I prefer a plain text file because it’s simple, reliable, and fast. I need speed when pumping out thoughts.
dd761683d7be.target: （用于离线使用）来追踪你独特的雪花脑袋中流淌出的想法。我更喜欢纯文本文件，因为它简单、可靠且快速。在迅速产出想法时我需要速度。

c557bf148c23.source: The more ideas I write down, the more I seem to have. I’m sure you’ll be the same.
c557bf148c23.target: 我记下的想法越多，似乎就拥有越多。我相信你也会一样。

6deacb59f1a1.source: I originally started by reading the experts, but found a lot of value from the feelgood and idea lists. I really value self-learning and I prefer these techniques.
6deacb59f1a1.target: 我最初从阅读专家开始，但从感觉良好和想法清单中发现了许多价值。我非常重视自学，并且更喜欢这些技巧。

06899f464a8e.source: But you’re reading this article, and I’m writing it, so I recognize the role of external tips and advice in getting started. Here’s what helped me:
06899f464a8e.target: 但你在读这篇文章，而我在写它，所以我认识到外部提示和建议在入门中的作用。以下是对我有帮助的内容：

0f40d19aa0fb.source: The point of personality tests isn’t to put you into a box. It’s to recognize the talents you have. The irony is that because
0f40d19aa0fb.target: 性格测试的目的不是把你放进一个盒子里。而是认识到你所拥有的才能。讽刺的是，因为

25db42d646ae.source: find something easy, we assume others do too, and therefore believe the skill is not valuable.
25db42d646ae.target: 发现某件事很容易，我们就认为别人也容易，因此相信这项技能没有价值。

0989db3235b6.source: It’s not always the case — some things that come easily to you (talking to strangers, using computers, doing math, writing, humor, relating to others) may be really hard for many other people.
0989db3235b6.target: 情况并非总是如此——对你来说很容易的一些事情（与陌生人交谈、使用电脑、做数学、写作、幽默、与他人相处）可能对许多其他人来说真的很难。

5153543e36e7.source: These tests can help you step back and realize what you do that others don’t.
5153543e36e7.target: 这些测试可以帮助你退一步，认识到你做了哪些别人做不到的事情。

f6699c4e123a.source: Sometimes it helps to remember why you are on this quest in the first place — to be happier and live a better life.
f6699c4e123a.target: 有时，记住你最初踏上这个旅程的原因会有帮助——变得更快乐，过上更好的生活。

bffca063ddf2.source: The wonderful and frustrating thing about understanding yourself is that nobody can do it for you. After a year of thinking about this seriously, I know what I like (learning and technology), what comes naturally to me (thinking about ideas and intuitive understanding) and what my ideas are about (improvements to software and education).
bffca063ddf2.target: 理解自己的美妙和令人沮丧之处在于，没有人能替你做到。经过一年的认真思考，我知道自己喜欢什么（学习和技术），什么对我来说是自然而然的（思考想法和直觉理解），以及我的想法是关于什么的（对软件和教育的改进）。

165efb1dd7d5.source: I had hunches about these things (obvious in hindsight, remember), but thinking about them solidly is very useful.
165efb1dd7d5.target: 我对这些事情有预感（事后看来很明显，记住），但扎实地思考它们非常有用。

908d71592d54.source: These insights aren’t set in stone – I don’t want them to be! Some of my greatest joys have been from seemingly random decisions and paths, like deciding to join karate or do toastmasters. These clues have given me a fulfilling direction to follow, and I’ll correct the course over time. Software happened to be the first field on the list of what I enjoy, and I’ll continue pursuing it as long as it keeps me excited.
908d71592d54.target: 这些见解并非一成不变——我也不希望它们如此！我的一些最大快乐来自于看似随意的决定和路径，比如决定加入空手道或参加演讲会。这些线索给了我一个充实的方向去追随，我会随着时间的推移调整方向。软件恰好是我喜欢的事物清单上的第一个领域，只要它能让我兴奋，我就会继续追求它。

765517728af2.source: There’ll be more articles on the mechanics of going on your own — a better understanding of what you want is a good staring point. Happy soul-searching.
765517728af2.target: 还会有更多关于独立前行的机制的文章——更好地理解你想要什么是一个好的起点。祝你内心探索愉快。

97721f00382a.target: 维基百科关于异或交换的文章

e0d7db89159c.source: XOR is a great way to combine information and later extract it. XOR-based encryption uses this technique. Also, XOR can combine N items together, not just 2.
e0d7db89159c.target: 异或是一种组合信息并随后提取信息的绝佳方式。基于异或的加密就使用了这项技术。此外，异或可以组合N个元素，而不仅仅是2个。

588dc8c33210.source: There are new ways to perform even the simplest operations.
588dc8c33210.target: 即使是最简单的操作，也有新的实现方式。

2f1c848b02f6.source: Most people would swap two variables x and y using a temporary variable, like this:
2f1c848b02f6.target: 大多数人会使用临时变量交换两个变量 x 和 y，像这样：

8fe32c248c62.source: Here’s a neat programming trick to swap two values without needing a temp:
8fe32c248c62.target: 这里有一个巧妙的编程技巧，无需临时变量即可交换两个值：

f8e63986b93e.source: Don’t believe me? Try it out – write in any initial value for x and y:
f8e63986b93e.target: 不相信？试试看 – 为 x 和 y 写入任意初始值：

1e6a7ac15d68.source: It almost seems like magic – the the same statement is repeated 3 times and
1e6a7ac15d68.target: 这几乎看起来像魔术 – 同样的语句重复了3次，然后

681b89579c77.source: – the values magically swap? Let’s take a closer look.
681b89579c77.target: – 这些值神奇地交换了？让我们仔细看看。

5866aecae1ee.source: To understand this trick, break the statements into unique values:
5866aecae1ee.target: 要理解这个技巧，把语句分解成唯一的值：

64a8f0f9d216.source: According to our code, x2 should have y’s original value. Let’s work out the details for the last step:
64a8f0f9d216.target: 根据我们的代码，x2 应该具有 y 的原始值。我们来详细计算最后一步：

eee97e03fd7b.source: Wow – x2 really does equal y! The swap happened. Now let’s try it out for y1:
eee97e03fd7b.target: 哇——x2 真的等于 y！交换成功了。现在让我们试试 y1：

5a136ed93d1d.source: And tada the trick worked again. x2 and y1 have the swapped answers.
5a136ed93d1d.target: 然后，这个技巧再次奏效。x2 和 y1 得到了交换后的值。

ee8df51fa575.source: Ok, sure, the boolean algebra works out great — but that’s not satisfying. I want to
ee8df51fa575.target: 好吧，当然，布尔代数运算得很好——但这不是令人满意的解释。我想

4455268ab703.source: it deep down and have it make sense, not be some artifact of the properties of XOR. Let’s take another look:
4455268ab703.target: 深入理解它，让它变得合理，而不是 XOR 属性的某种产物。让我们再看一看：

d49ceb5820ce.source: On line 1 we combine x and y (using XOR) to get this “hybrid” and we store it back in x. XOR is a great way to save information, because you can remove it by doing an XOR again.
d49ceb5820ce.target: 在第 1 行，我们将 x 和 y（使用 XOR）组合起来，得到这个“混合体”，并将其存回 x。XOR 是保存信息的好方法，因为你可以再次进行 XOR 来移除它。

6b380e0cbdcd.source: So, this is exactly what we do on line 2. We XOR the hybrid with y, which cancels out all the y information, leaving us only with x. We save this result back into y, so now they have swapped.
6b380e0cbdcd.target: 所以，这正是我们在第 2 行所做的。我们将混合体与 y 进行 XOR，这会消除所有 y 的信息，只留下 x。我们将这个结果存回 y，现在它们交换了。

109f9ea24789.source: On the last line, x still has the hybrid value. We XOR it yet again with y (now with x’s original value) to remove all traces of x out of the hybrid. This leaves us with y, and the swap is complete!
109f9ea24789.target: 在最后一行，x 仍然有混合体的值。我们再次将其与 y（现在有 x 的原始值）进行 XOR，以从混合体中消除 x 的所有痕迹。这留下 y，交换完成！

ae3d5b891067.source: No way. This is a cool trick, but don’t write this as an actual swap function. If you have to debug it in 6 months you’ll be in for some fun. Let me show you why:
ae3d5b891067.target: 不行。这是一个很酷的技巧，但不要把它写成一个实际的交换函数。如果你在 6 个月后需要调试它，你会遇到一些麻烦。让我告诉你为什么：

9687cd69ecb5.source: Suppose x and y are pointers or references to objects, and both point to the same location. We’d expect our swapping function to just switch the values around, with no net change, right?
9687cd69ecb5.target: 假设 x 和 y 是指向对象的指针或引用，并且两者指向同一位置。我们期望交换函数只是交换值，没有净变化，对吧？

8340d0175397.source: Well, take a look at what happens if we expand out line 1:
8340d0175397.target: 好吧，看看如果我们展开第 1 行会发生什么：

57dadb1df9c0.source: Wow! So x becomes 0 right at the get-go. That’s ok by itself, but because x and y are at the same location, we just made y zero as well! We’ve lost the original values, a problem known as
57dadb1df9c0.target: 哇！所以 x 一开始就变成了 0。这本身没问题，但因为 x 和 y 在同一位置，我们也让 y 变成了 0！我们丢失了原始值，这个问题被称为

a1f60578f7da.source: : changing one variable has an indirect effect on another.
a1f60578f7da.target: 副作用：改变一个变量会对另一个变量产生间接影响。

18fd30f83e53.source: So, would you have caught this bug? I wouldn’t have, and it would have been a nightmare figuring out why an innocuous swap function was causing data loss. Cute tricks like these can be pretty dangerous. As Brian Kernighan said:
18fd30f83e53.target: 那么，你能发现这个 bug 吗？我本来不会，而且找出为什么一个无害的交换函数会导致数据丢失将是一场噩梦。像这样的小技巧可能相当危险。正如 Brian Kernighan 所说：

25bf9c896af3.source: Debugging is twice as hard as writing the code in the first place. Therefore, if you write the code as cleverly as possible, you are, by definition, not smart enough to debug it.
25bf9c896af3.target: 调试代码比编写代码要难一倍。因此，如果你尽可能巧妙地编写代码，那么按定义，你就不够聪明去调试它。

3dfd8516d67d.source: This applies so well here – we wrote the code pretty much as cleverly as we could :). So, treat this as an intellectual exercise that brings up a few points:
3dfd8516d67d.target: 这在这里非常适用——我们尽可能巧妙地编写了代码 :)。所以，把它当作一次智力练习，提出几点：

4979ceae086d.source: Now, how does this work on a CPU-level?
4979ceae086d.target: 现在，这在 CPU 层面是如何工作的？

22a1e61749e0.source: The computer actually has an implicit “temp” variable that stores intermediate results before writing them back to a register. For example, if you add 3 to a register (in machine-language pseudocode):
22a1e61749e0.target: 计算机实际上有一个隐式的“临时”变量，在将中间结果写回寄存器之前存储它们。例如，如果你向寄存器加 3（用机器语言伪代码）：

f4008b04e99d.source: (Arithmetic Logic Unit) is actually what executes the instruction 3+A. It takes the inputs (3,A) and creates a result (3 + A), which the CPU then stores back into A’s original register. So, we used the ALU as temporary scratch space before we had the final answer.
f4008b04e99d.target: (算术逻辑单元)实际上执行指令3+A。它接收输入(3,A)并产生结果(3 + A)，然后CPU将结果存回A的原始寄存器。因此，在得到最终答案之前，我们将ALU用作临时暂存空间。

a51772b820a5.source: We take the ALU’s implicit temporary data for granted, but it’s always there. In a similar way, the ALU can return the intermediate result of the XOR in the case of
a51772b820a5.target: 我们理所当然地认为ALU有隐式的临时数据，但它始终存在。类似地，在

9a5c19eefd50.source: , at which point the CPU stores it into x’s original register.
9a5c19eefd50.target: 的情况下，ALU可以返回XOR的中间结果，此时CPU将其存储到x的原始寄存器中。

446960250e4c.source: Because we aren’t used to thinking about the poor, neglected ALU, the XOR swap seems magical because it doesn’t have an explicit temporary variable.  Some machines have a 1-step exchange XCHG instruction to swap two registers.
446960250e4c.target: 因为我们不习惯考虑那个被忽视的、可怜的ALU，所以XOR交换看起来神奇，因为它没有显式的临时变量。有些机器有一条一步交换的XCHG指令，用于交换两个寄存器。

23c83d2f020f.target: 函数在点x=0附近的泰勒级数为：

0d803e676458.source: The generalized Taylor series, extracted from any point a is:
0d803e676458.target: 从任意点a出发的广义泰勒级数为：

4a63eb852043.source: 2) Sine is missing every other term
4a63eb852043.target: 2) 正弦函数每隔一项缺失

1502dfcb8a07.source: 3) Different starting positions have different DNA
1502dfcb8a07.target: 3) 不同的起始位置有不同的DNA

ad256ae02e24.source: Does the Taylor Series always work?

77d3cbafe7a0.target: 将几何定义转化为代数定义

cc1d3b478d94.source: $f'(x)$ = First derivative, or how fast the function is changing (the velocity)
cc1d3b478d94.target: $f'(x)$ = 一阶导数，表示函数变化的速度（速度）

1a56574cd993.source: $f''(x)$ = Second derivative, or how fast the

f485f67cc75c.source: $f'''(x)$ = Third derivative, or how fast the

93b5f9232ea8.source: in the changes are changing (acceleration of the acceleration)
93b5f9232ea8.target: 变化的变化的速度（加速度的加速度）

4a13ae5cb51f.source: The $O(x^{12})$ term means there are other components of order (power) $x^{12}$ and higher. Because $\sin(x)$ has infinite derivatives, we have infinite terms and the computer has to cut us off somewhere. (
4a13ae5cb51f.target: $O(x^{12})$ 项表示存在其他阶（幂次）为 $x^{12}$ 及更高的分量。因为 $\sin(x)$ 有无穷多阶导数，所以有无穷多项，计算机必须在某处截断。（

b9d8b3092b9d.source: $c_0 = f(0)$, the constant term, is the exact value at the point
b9d8b3092b9d.target: $c_0 = f(0)$，常数项，是函数在该点的精确值

e3fe4f2ea4ca.source: $c_1 = f'(0)x$, the linear term, tells us what speed to move from our point
e3fe4f2ea4ca.target: $c_1 = f'(0)x$，线性项，告诉我们要从该点以什么速度移动

1ffb599cb1bb.source: $c_2= \frac{f''(0)}{2!}x^2 $, the quadratic term, tells us how much to accelerate away from our point
1ffb599cb1bb.target: $c_2= \frac{f''(0)}{2!}x^2 $，二次项，告诉我们从该点加速离开的程度

88db28ed9b0b.source: Growing a Function from a point

8173a1207bf9.source: You've had enough Tayloring for today, buddy.
8173a1207bf9.target: 今天你受够了泰勒展开(Tayloring)了，伙计。

c7fdae71fbbb.source: Your body has a strange property: you can learn information about the
c7fdae71fbbb.target: 你的身体有一个奇特的特性：你可以从单个细胞中获取关于该有机体的信息。

23b6a47eb658.source: organism from a single cell. Pick a cell, dive into the nucleus, and extract the DNA. You can now regrow the entire creature from that tiny sample.
23b6a47eb658.target: 从单个细胞中了解有机体。挑一个细胞，潜入细胞核，提取DNA。现在你就可以从那个微小的样本中重新培育出整个生物。

842634e82f55.source: There's a math analogy here. Take a function, pick a specific point, and dive in. You can pull out enough data from a single point to rebuild the entire function. Whoa. It's like remaking a movie from a single frame.
842634e82f55.target: 这里有一个数学类比。取一个函数，选定一个具体点，然后深入进去。你可以从一个点中提取足够的数据来重建整个函数。哇，就像从一帧画面重新制作整部电影一样。

9f141fead073.source: The Taylor Series discovers the "math DNA" behind a function and lets us rebuild it from a single data point. Let's see how it works.
9f141fead073.target: 泰勒级数(Taylor Series)发现了函数背后的"数学DNA"，让我们能够从单个数据点重建它。让我们看看它是如何工作的。

51db48543dc6.source: Given a function like $f(x) = x^2$, what can we discover at a single location?
51db48543dc6.target: 给定一个函数如$f(x) = x^2$，我们可以在单个位置发现什么？

6ef10985a9b2.source: Normally we'd expect to calculate a single value, like $f(4) = 16$. But there's much more beneath the surface:
6ef10985a9b2.target: 通常我们期望计算一个单一的值，比如$f(4) = 16$。但表面之下还有更多：

9f737ffeab13.source: Investigating a single point reveals multiple, possibly infinite, bits of information about the behavior. (Some functions have an endless amount of data (derivatives) at a single point).
9f737ffeab13.target: 研究一个点会揭示关于行为的多个（可能是无限的）信息片段。（有些函数在一个点上有无穷多的数据（导数(derivative)）。）

14a03ab1e43b.source: So, given all this information, what should we do? Regrow the organism from a single cell, of course! (
14a03ab1e43b.target: 那么，有了所有这些信息，我们该怎么做？当然是从一个细胞重新培育有机体！（

4ae0451af974.source: Our plan is to grow a function from a single starting point. But how can we describe any function in a generic way?
4ae0451af974.target: 我们的计划是从一个起点开始生长出一个函数。但我们如何通用地描述任意函数呢？

89343cfdc81e.source: The big aha moment: imagine any function, at its core, is a polynomial (with possibly infinite terms):
89343cfdc81e.target: 顿悟时刻：想象任何函数，其核心都是一个多项式（可能有无穷多项）：

4802ab7bcf5f.source: To rebuild our function, we start at a fixed point ($c_0$) and add in a bunch of other terms based on the value we feed it (like $c_1x$). The "DNA" is the values $c_0, c_1, c_2, c_3$ that describe our function exactly.
4802ab7bcf5f.target: 为了重建我们的函数，我们从固定点($c_0$)开始，并根据输入的值添加一系列其他项（如$c_1x$）。"DNA"就是精确描述我们函数的$c_0, c_1, c_2, c_3$这些值。

0f15b67066e4.source: Ok, we have a generic "function format". But how do we find the coefficients for a specific function like
0f15b67066e4.target: 好了，我们有了一个通用的"函数格式"。但如何找到特定函数的系数，比如

bebecb5636ce.source: (height of angle x on the unit circle)? How do we pull out its DNA?
bebecb5636ce.target: （单位圆上角度x的高度）？如何提取它的DNA？

736324db8354.source: Let's start by plugging in the function value at $x=0$. Doing this, we get:
736324db8354.target: 让我们从代入$x=0$处的函数值开始。这样做，我们得到：

dc1a80f41524.source: Every term vanishes except $c_0$, which makes sense: the starting point of our blueprint should be $f(0)$. For $f(x) = \sin(x)$, we can work out $c_0 = \sin(0) = 0$. We have our first bit of DNA!
dc1a80f41524.target: 除了$c_0$之外所有项都消失了，这很合理：我们蓝图的起点应该是$f(0)$。对于$f(x) = \sin(x)$，我们可以算出$c_0 = \sin(0) = 0$。我们得到了第一段DNA！

dd53d4a1d5c3.source: Now that we know $c_0$, how do we isolate $c_1$ in this equation?
dd53d4a1d5c3.target: 既然我们知道$c_0$，如何在这个方程中分离出$c_1$？

f8eabeed17af.source: Can we set $x = 1$? That gives $f(1) = c_0 + c_1(1) + c_2(1^2) + c_3(1^3) + \cdots$ . Although we know $c_0$, the other constants are summed together. We can't pull out $c_1$ by itself.
f8eabeed17af.target: 我们可以令 $x = 1$ 吗？这样得到 $f(1) = c_0 + c_1(1) + c_2(1^2) + c_3(1^3) + \cdots$。虽然我们知道 $c_0$，但其他常数都加在一起了，无法单独分离出 $c_1$。

788f70b8696a.source: What if we divide by $x$? This gives:
788f70b8696a.target: 如果我们除以 $x$ 会怎样？得到：

0de7312f30ef.source: Then we can set $x=0$ to make the other terms disappear... right? It's a nice idea, except we're now dividing by zero.
0de7312f30ef.target: 然后我们可以令 $x=0$ 让其他项消失……对吗？这个想法不错，但问题是我们现在除以了零。

1f8c7ff32899.source: Hrm. This approach is really close. How can we
1f8c7ff32899.target: 嗯。这个方法已经很接近了。我们怎样才能

081f3c1f32e4.source: If we take the derivative of the blueprint of $f(x)$, we get:
081f3c1f32e4.target: 如果我们对 $f(x)$ 的蓝图求导，得到：

bd5e8d7eedc5.source: Every power gets reduced by 1 and the $c_0$, a constant value, becomes zero. It's almost too convenient.
bd5e8d7eedc5.target: 每个幂次都减 1，而常数项 $c_0$ 变为零。这简直太方便了。

e347bf47c715.source: Now we can isolate $c_1$ using our $x=0$ trick:
e347bf47c715.target: 现在我们可以用之前的 $x=0$ 技巧来分离出 $c_1$：

ff1cc5f259d7.source: In our example, $\sin'(x) = \cos(x)$ so we compute: $f'(0) = \sin'(0) = \cos(0) = 1 = c_1$
ff1cc5f259d7.target: 在我们的例子中，$\sin'(x) = \cos(x)$，所以我们计算：$f'(0) = \sin'(0) = \cos(0) = 1 = c_1$

8f4abe1d3dec.source: Yay, one more bit of DNA! This is the magic of the Taylor series: by repeatedly applying the derivative and setting $x = 0$, we can pull out the polynomial DNA.
8f4abe1d3dec.target: 耶，又多了一点点 DNA！这就是泰勒级数的魔力：通过反复求导并令 $x = 0$，我们可以提取出多项式的 DNA。

c11d175f6a22.source: After taking the second derivative, the powers are reduced again. The first two terms ($c_0$ and $c_1x$) disappear, and we can again isolate $c_2$ by setting $x=0$:
c11d175f6a22.target: 取二阶导数后，幂次再次降低。前两项（$c_0$ 和 $c_1x$）消失了，我们可以再次令 $x=0$ 来分离出 $c_2$：

3e811d073f4b.target: 对于我们的正弦例子，$\sin'' = -\sin$，所以：

01e1138296c4.source: As we keep taking derivatives, we're performing more multiplications and growing a factorial in front of each term (1!, 2!, 3!).
01e1138296c4.target: 随着我们不断求导，我们执行了更多乘法，每个项前面会累积一个阶乘（1!，2!，3!）。

7c64a2e5d6f7.source: (Formally, the Taylor series around the point $x=0$ is called the MacLaurin series.)
7c64a2e5d6f7.target: （严格来说，在点 $x=0$ 附近的泰勒级数称为麦克劳林级数。）

295413dfe785.source: The idea is the same. Instead of our regular blueprint, we use:
295413dfe785.target: 思路是一样的。我们不再使用常规蓝图，而是使用：

6a3bf986033e.source: Since we're growing from $f(a)$, we can see that $f(a) = c_0 + 0 + 0 + \dots = c_0$. The other coefficients can be extracted by taking derivatives and setting $x = a$ (instead of $x =0$).
6a3bf986033e.target: 因为我们是从 $f(a)$ 展开，所以 $f(a) = c_0 + 0 + 0 + \dots = c_0$。其他系数可以通过求导并令 $x = a$（而不是 $x=0$）来提取。

9d308f1db111.source: Plugging in derivatives into the formula above, here's the Taylor series of $\sin(x)$ around $x = 0$:
9d308f1db111.target: 将导数代入上述公式，得到 $\sin(x)$ 在 $x = 0$ 附近的泰勒级数：

ef0be335ef3d.source: And here's what that looks like:

13b4939e6664.source: Sine is an infinite wave, and as you can guess, needs an infinite number of terms to keep it going. Simpler functions (like $f(x) = x^2 + 3$) are already in their "polynomial format" and don't have infinite derivatives to keep the DNA going.
13b4939e6664.target: 正弦(Sine)是一个无穷波，正如你所猜测的，它需要无穷多项才能持续下去。更简单的函数（如 $f(x) = x^2 + 3$）已经处于它们的“多项式格式”中，不需要无穷阶导数来维持DNA。

13f1c935f067.source: If we repeatedly take the derivative of sine at x = 0 we get:
13f1c935f067.target: 如果我们反复对正弦在 x = 0 处求导，得到：

99781cad94da.source: Ignoring the division by the factorial, we get the pattern:
99781cad94da.target: 忽略除以阶乘，我们得到模式：

9a14d5ca5a97.source: So the DNA of sine is something like [0, 1, 0, -1] repeating.
9a14d5ca5a97.target: 因此正弦的DNA类似于 [0, 1, 0, -1] 重复。

e9d86b2db834.source: For fun, here's the Taylor series of $\sin(x)$ starting at $x =\pi$ (
e9d86b2db834.target: 作为趣味，这里是 $\sin(x)$ 在 $x =\pi$ 处的泰勒级数(Taylor series)（

1ade71646211.source: The DNA is now something like [0, -1, 0, 1]. The cycle is similar, but the starting value has changed since we're starting at $x=\pi$.
1ade71646211.target: 现在DNA类似于 [0, -1, 0, 1]。循环类似，但由于我们从 $x=\pi$ 开始，起始值发生了变化。

9d29a9c32813.source: Written as calculated numbers, the denominators 1, 6, 120, 5040 look strange. But they're just every other factorial: 1! = 1, 3! = 6, 5! =120, 7! = 5040. In general, the Taylor series can have gnarly denominators.
9d29a9c32813.target: 写成计算后的数字，分母 1, 6, 120, 5040 看起来很奇怪。但它们只是每隔一个阶乘：1! = 1, 3! = 6, 5! =120, 7! = 5040。一般来说，泰勒级数的分母可能很复杂。

f392547c6fc2.source: A popular use of Taylor series is getting a quick approximation for a function. If you want a tadpole, do you need the DNA for the entire frog?
f392547c6fc2.target: 泰勒级数的一个常见用途是快速近似一个函数。如果你想要一只蝌蚪，你需要整只青蛙的DNA吗？

92965262a59b.source: The Taylor series has a bunch of terms, typically ordered by importance:
92965262a59b.target: 泰勒级数有一系列项，通常按重要性排序：

dedb8cd1af13.source: If we only need a prediction for a few instants around our point, the initial position & velocity may be good enough:
dedb8cd1af13.target: 如果我们只需要预测点周围几个瞬间的情况，初始位置和速度可能就足够了：

4ed61e1d5a77.source: If we're tracking for longer, then acceleration becomes important:
4ed61e1d5a77.target: 如果我们要追踪更长时间，那么加速度(Acceleration)就变得重要了：

395319478fc1.source: As we get further from our starting point, we need more terms to keep our prediction accurate. For example, the linear model $\sin(x) = x$ is a good prediction around $x=0$. As we get further out, we need to account for more terms.
395319478fc1.target: 随着我们远离起始点，我们需要更多项来保持预测的准确性。例如，线性模型 $\sin(x) = x$ 在 $x=0$ 附近是一个好的预测。随着我们走得更远，我们需要考虑更多项。

748721953f0a.source: Similarly, $e^x \sim 1 + x$ works well for small interest rates: 1% discrete interest is 1.01 after one time period, 1% continuous interest is a tad higher than 1.01. As time goes on, the linear model falls behind because it ignores the compounding effects.
748721953f0a.target: 类似地，$e^x \sim 1 + x$ 对于小利率效果很好：1% 离散利率在一个时间周期后是 1.01，1% 连续利率比 1.01 略高。随着时间的推移，线性模型落后了，因为它忽略了复利效应(Compounding Effects)。

dd68df5e3a1b.source: What's a common application of DNA? Paternity tests.
dd68df5e3a1b.target: DNA的一个常见应用是什么？亲子鉴定(Paternity Tests)。

e0945f3be098.source: If we have a few functions, we can compare their Taylor series to see if they're related.
e0945f3be098.target: 如果我们有几个函数，可以比较它们的泰勒级数，看看它们是否相关。

805cc1dbffa7.target: 以下是 $\sin(x)$、$\cos(x)$ 和 $e^x$ 的展开式：

5b1878ec7348.source: There's a family resemblence in the sequences, right? Clean powers of $x$ divided by a factorial?
5b1878ec7348.target: 这些序列有家族相似性，对吧？干净的 $x$ 的幂除以阶乘？

780153f9e31c.source: One problem is the sequence for $e^x$ has positive terms, while sine and cosine alternate signs. How can we link these together?
780153f9e31c.target: 一个问题是 $e^x$ 的序列项都是正的，而正弦和余弦交替符号。我们如何将它们联系起来？

5463f5c40f3a.source: Euler's great insight was realizing an
5463f5c40f3a.target: 欧拉(Euler)的伟大洞察在于意识到一个

a50d6c54b07f.source: could swap the sign from positive to negative:
a50d6c54b07f.target: 可以将符号从正号改为负号：

8c63bc91693d.source: Whoa. Using an imaginary exponent and separating into odd/even powers reveals that sine and cosine are hiding inside the exponential function. Amazing.
8c63bc91693d.target: 哇。使用虚数指数并分解为奇/偶次幂，揭示了正弦和余弦其实隐藏在指数函数中。太神奇了。

2c1929cca960.source: the imaginary number makes sense, it reveals the baby daddy hiding backstage.
2c1929cca960.target: 虚数是有意义的，但它揭示了幕后隐藏的“老祖宗”。

04c2bb6c5f15.source: The Taylor Series extracts the "polynomial DNA" and the
04c2bb6c5f15.target: 泰勒级数提取了函数的“多项式DNA”，而

e4e6f28a9ba5.source: extracts the "circular DNA" of a function. Both see functions as built from smaller parts (polynomials or exponential paths).
e4e6f28a9ba5.target: 提取了函数的“圆形DNA”。两者都将函数视为由更小的部分（多项式或指数路径）构建而成。

bb3e5c73e2fe.source: This gets into mathematical analysis beyond my depth, but certain functions aren't easily (or ever) approximated with polynomials.
bb3e5c73e2fe.target: 这涉及到我能力范围之外的数学分析，但某些函数不容易（甚至永远不能）用多项式近似。

aa9bc365b0ae.source: Notice that powers like $x^2, x^3$ explode as $x$ grows. In order to have a slow, gradual curve, you need an army of polynomial terms fighting it out, with one winner barely emerging. If you stop the train too early, the approximation explodes again.
aa9bc365b0ae.target: 注意，像 $x^2, x^3$ 这样的幂次随着 $x$ 增长而爆炸。为了获得缓慢、渐进的曲线，你需要一支多项式项组成的军队来相互竞争，仅有一个获胜者勉强胜出。如果你过早停止训练，近似值又会爆炸。

7290f3f50e78.source: For example, here's the Taylor Series for $\ln(1 + x)$. The black line is the curve we want, and adding more terms, even dozens, barely gets us accuracy beyond $x=1.0$. It's just too hard to maintain a gentle slope with terms that want to run hog wild.
7290f3f50e78.target: 例如，这里是 $\ln(1 + x)$ 的泰勒级数。黑线是我们想要的曲线，添加更多项，甚至几十项，也只能勉强在 $x=1.0$ 附近获得精确度。用这些想要疯狂失控的项来维持平缓斜率实在太难了。

d7d5a5a36221.source: In this case, we only have a radius of convergence where the approximation stays accurate (such as around $|x| < 1$).
d7d5a5a36221.target: 在这种情况下，我们只有一个收敛半径，近似值在此半径内保持准确（例如在 $|x| < 1$ 附近）。

635ebc0be0bc.source: Sine is often defined geometrically: the height of a line on a circular figure.
635ebc0be0bc.target: 正弦通常用几何定义：圆图形上一条线的高度。

2a94892145b0.source: Turning this into an equation seems really hard. The Taylor Series gives us a process: If we know a single value and how it changes (the derivative), we can reverse-engineer the DNA.
2a94892145b0.target: 将其转化为方程似乎非常困难。泰勒级数给了我们一个过程：如果我们知道单个值及其变化方式（导数），我们就可以反向工程出DNA。

fe033d38f53b.source: Similarly, the description of $e^x$ as "the function with its derivative equal to the current value" yields the DNA [1, 1, 1, 1], and polynomial $f(x) = 1 + \frac{1}{1!}x + \frac{1}{2!}x^2 + \frac{1}{3!}x^3 + \dots $. We went from a verbal description to an equation.
fe033d38f53b.target: 类似地，将 $e^x$ 描述为“导数等于当前值的函数”产生了DNA [1, 1, 1, 1] 和多项式 $f(x) = 1 + \frac{1}{1!}x + \frac{1}{2!}x^2 + \frac{1}{3!}x^3 + \dots $。我们从一个文字描述得到了一个方程。

599f40fb037e.source: Phew! A few items to ponder.

9cab87f359c9.target: 一千的平方等于一百万

0ec2f46b703e.source: the sum of each column is 11

c13157cd0374.source: Instead of 1 to n, how about 5 to n?
c13157cd0374.target: 如果不是从1到n，而是从5到n呢？

c2a00a0ca7b5.source: How about even numbers, like 2 + 4 + 6 + 8 + … + n?
c2a00a0ca7b5.target: 如果是偶数呢，比如2 + 4 + 6 + 8 + … + n？

e56a1fccc604.source: How about odd numbers, like 1 + 3 + 5 + 7 + … + n?
e56a1fccc604.target: 如果是奇数呢，比如1 + 3 + 5 + 7 + … + n？

babbd34bd249.source: Wait — what about an odd number of items?
babbd34bd249.target: 等等——如果有奇数个数字会怎样？

afb2bb59cddf.target: 有一个流传很广的故事，关于

085d1621cb39.source: , mathematician extraordinaire, had a lazy teacher. The so-called educator wanted to keep the kids busy so he could take a nap; he asked the class to add the numbers 1 to 100.
085d1621cb39.target: ，数学家中的非凡人物，他有一位懒惰的老师。这位所谓的教育家想让学生们忙碌起来以便自己打个盹；他让全班同学计算从1加到100的和。

9e0748f88eae.source: Gauss approached with his answer: 5050. So soon? The teacher suspected a cheat, but no. Manual addition was for suckers, and Gauss found a formula to sidestep the problem:
9e0748f88eae.target: 高斯很快给出了答案：5050。这么快？老师怀疑有作弊行为，但并非如此。手动加法是傻子才做的事，高斯找到了一个绕过问题的公式：

d57e33a33107.source: Let’s share a few explanations of this result and really understand it intuitively. For these examples we’ll add 1 to 10, and then see how it applies for 1 to 100 (or 1 to any number).
d57e33a33107.target: 让我们分享几个关于这个结果的解释，并真正直观地理解它。对于这些例子，我们将计算从1加到10，然后看看它如何应用于1到100（或任意数字）。

c76896c378ed.source: Pairing numbers is a common approach to this problem. Instead of writing all the numbers in a single column, let’s wrap the numbers around, like this:
c76896c378ed.target: 配对数字是解决这个问题的常见方法。与其将所有数字写在一列中，不如把数字绕过来，像这样：

07349fdde7c9.target: 一个有趣的模式出现了：

581e933f556f.source: . As the top row increases, the bottom row decreases, so the sum stays the same.
581e933f556f.target: 。随着第一行增加，第二行减少，因此和保持不变。

c39a5697f3ee.source: Because 1 is paired with 10 (our n), we can say that each column has (n+1). And how many pairs do we have? Well, we have 2 equal rows, we must have n/2 pairs.
c39a5697f3ee.target: 因为1与10（我们的n）配对，所以我们可以说每列的和为(n+1)。那么我们有多少对呢？嗯，我们有2行相等的行，因此必须有n/2对。

ce673bf52608.source: Ah, I’m glad you brought it up. What if we are adding up the numbers 1 to 9? We don’t have an even number of items to pair up. Many explanations will just give the explanation above and leave it at that. I won’t.
ce673bf52608.target: 啊，我很高兴你提到这一点。如果我们计算从1加到9呢？我们就没有偶数个项目可以配对了。许多解释只会给出上述解释并就此打住。但我不会。

2b00abd59e26.source: Let’s add the numbers 1 to 9, but instead of starting from 1, let’s count from 0 instead:
2b00abd59e26.target: 让我们计算从1加到9，但这次不从1开始，而是从0开始计数：

825d61968ce0.source: By counting from 0, we get an “extra item” (10 in total) so we can have an even number of rows. However, our formula will look a bit different.
825d61968ce0.target: 通过从0开始计数，我们得到了一个“额外项”（总共10个），这样我们就有偶数行了。不过，我们的公式会略有不同。

010f2fd5d34d.source: Notice that each column has a sum of n (not n+1, like before), since 0 and 9 are grouped. And instead of having exactly n items in 2 rows (for n/2 pairs total), we have n + 1 items in 2 rows (for (n + 1)/2 pairs total). If you plug these numbers in you get:
010f2fd5d34d.target: 注意每列的和是n（不像之前是n+1），因为0和9被分在一组。而且我们不是正好有2行中的n个项（总共n/2对），而是有2行中的n+1个项（总共(n+1)/2对）。如果你将这些数字代入，你会得到：

8f85e55619be.source: which is the same formula as before. It always bugged me that the same formula worked for both odd and even numbers – won’t you get a fraction? Yep, you get the same formula, but for different reasons.
8f85e55619be.target: 这和之前的公式是一样的。我一直觉得奇怪的是，同一个公式既适用于奇数也适用于偶数——难道不会得到分数吗？是的，你会得到相同的公式，但原因不同。

771b42d07af8.source: The above method works, but you handle odd and even numbers differently. Isn’t there a better way? Yes.
771b42d07af8.target: 上述方法可行，但你处理奇数和偶数的方式不同。难道没有更好的方法吗？有的。

7b9a28539af8.source: Instead of looping the numbers around, let’s write them in two rows:
7b9a28539af8.target: 与其将数字绕圈排列，不如将它们写成两行：

64a9b0a69832.source: Notice that we have 10 pairs, and each pair adds up to 10+1.
64a9b0a69832.target: 注意我们有10对，每对加起来等于10+1。

e584a9387a21.source: The total of all the numbers above is
e584a9387a21.target: 以上所有数字的总和是

747fa0b7a116.source: But we only want the sum of one row, not both. So we divide the formula above by 2 and get:
747fa0b7a116.target: 但我们只想要一行的总和，而不是两行。因此，我们将上述公式除以2得到：

7fff4284a459.source: Now this is cool (as cool as rows of numbers can be). It works for an odd or even number of items the same!
7fff4284a459.target: 这很酷（就数字行而言）。它对奇数或偶数个项同样适用！

1744e9ad5b51.source: I recently stumbled upon another explanation, a fresh approach to the old pairing explanation. Different explanations work better for different people, and I tend to like this one better.
1744e9ad5b51.target: 我最近偶然发现了另一种解释，一种对旧配对解释的新方法。不同的解释对不同的人更有效，我倾向于更喜欢这个。

ebe0622bf5a8.source: Instead of writing out numbers, pretend we have beans. We want to add 1 bean to 2 beans to 3 beans… all the way up to 5 beans.
ebe0622bf5a8.target: 与其写出数字，不如假设我们有豆子。我们想把1个豆子加到2个豆子、3个豆子……一直到5个豆子。

8dd75e41ff3c.source: Sure, we could go to 10 or 100 beans, but with 5 you get the idea. How do we count the number of beans in our pyramid?
8dd75e41ff3c.target: 当然，我们可以加到10或100个豆子，但用5个你就能明白。如何计算我们金字塔中的豆子数量？

b34c50fff996.source: Well, the sum is clearly 1 + 2 + 3 + 4 + 5. But let’s look at it a different way. Let’s say we mirror our pyramid (I’ll use “o” for the mirrored beans), and then topple it over:
b34c50fff996.target: 嗯，总和显然是1 + 2 + 3 + 4 + 5。但让我们换个角度来看。假设我们镜像我们的金字塔（我用“o”表示镜像的豆子），然后将其翻倒：

aaa0c5a6631b.source: Cool, huh? In case you’re wondering whether it “really” lines up, it does. Take a look at the bottom row of the regular pyramid, with 5′x (and 1 o). The next row of the pyramid has 1 less x (4 total) and 1 more o (2 total) to fill the gap. Just like the pairing, one side is increasing, and the other is decreasing.
aaa0c5a6631b.target: 很酷吧？如果你想知道它是否“真的”对齐，确实如此。看一下常规金字塔的底部行，有5个x（和1个o）。金字塔的下一行少1个x（总共4个）和多1个o（总共2个）来填补空隙。就像配对一样，一侧在增加，另一侧在减少。

8875327ad63e.source: Now for the explanation: How many beans do we have total? Well, that’s just the area of the rectangle.
8875327ad63e.target: 现在来解释：我们总共有多少豆子？嗯，那只是矩形的面积。

84568d92c575.source: We have n rows (we didn’t change the number of rows in the pyramid), and our collection is (n + 1) units wide, since 1 “o” is paired up with all the “x”s.
84568d92c575.target: 我们有n行（我们没有改变金字塔的行数），我们的集合宽度为（n + 1）个单位，因为1个“o”与所有“x”配对。

bc3839d59480.source: Notice that this time, we don’t care about n being odd or even – the total area formula works out just fine. If n is odd, we’ll have an even number of items (n+1) in each row.
bc3839d59480.target: 注意，这次我们不再关心n是奇数还是偶数——总面积公式同样适用。如果n是奇数，每行将有偶数个项（n+1）。

7b7efa16980b.source: But of course, we don’t want the total area (the number of x’s and o’s), we just want the number of x’s. Since we doubled the x’s to get the o’s, the x’s by themselves are just half of the total area:
7b7efa16980b.target: 但当然，我们不想要总面积（x和o的数量），我们只想要x的数量。由于我们将x加倍得到o，所以x本身只是总面积的一半：

659ae86504af.source: And we’re back to our original formula. Again, the number of x’s in the pyramid = 1 + 2 + 3 + 4 + 5, or the sum from 1 to n.
659ae86504af.target: 我们又回到了原来的公式。再次强调，金字塔中x的数量 = 1 + 2 + 3 + 4 + 5，即从1到n的和。

6012b0c95e15.source: So let’s figure out the sum. If we have 100 numbers (1…100), then we clearly have 100 items. That was easy.
6012b0c95e15.target: 那么让我们来计算总和。如果我们有100个数字（1…100），那么我们显然有100个项。这很容易。

ea5d2cbb1159.source: To get the average, notice that the numbers are all equally distributed. For every big number, there’s a small number on the other end. Let’s look at a small set:
ea5d2cbb1159.target: 为了得到平均值，注意这些数字是均匀分布的。对于每个大数字，另一端都有一个小数字。让我们看一个小集合：

8961d048e923.source: The average is 2. 2 is already in the middle, and 1 and 3 “cancel out” so their average is 2.
8961d048e923.target: 平均值是2。2已经在中间，1和3“相互抵消”，所以它们的平均值是2。

cd46030dec2e.source: For an even number of items

6dcd7d2485c2.source: the average is between 2 and 3 – it’s 2.5. Even though we have a fractional average, this is ok — since we have an
6dcd7d2485c2.target: 平均值介于2和3之间——是2.5。即使我们有一个分数平均值，这也没问题——因为我们有

ce301d3443dc.source: number of items, when we multiply the average by the count that ugly fraction will disappear.
ce301d3443dc.target: 项目数量，当我们用平均值乘以计数时，那个丑陋的分数就会消失。

4ac64952d3ca.source: Notice in both cases, 1 is on one side of the average and N is equally far away on the other. So, we can say the average of the entire set is actually just the average of 1 and n: (1 + n)/2.
4ac64952d3ca.target: 注意在这两种情况下，1 在平均值的一侧，而 N 在另一侧等距。所以，我们可以说整个集合的平均值实际上就是 1 和 n 的平均值：(1 + n)/2。

0206e5de9fb9.source: And voila! We have a fourth way of thinking about our formula.
0206e5de9fb9.target: 瞧！我们有了理解公式的第四种方式。

6a91144861ca.source: 1) Adding up numbers quickly can be useful for estimation. Notice that the formula expands to this:
6a91144861ca.target: 1) 快速累加数字对估算很有用。注意，公式展开为：

9209e22ef7e5.source: Let’s say you want to add the numbers from 1 to 1000: suppose you get 1 additional visitor to your site each day – how many total visitors will you have after 1000 days? Since
9209e22ef7e5.target: 假设你想把从 1 到 1000 的数字加起来：假设你的网站每天增加 1 个访客——1000 天后你总共有多少访客？由于

0729a9f3d759.source: 2) This concept of adding numbers 1 to N shows up in other places, like figuring out the probability for the
0729a9f3d759.target: 2) 这种将 1 到 N 相加的概念也出现在其他地方，例如计算概率时用于

439e25d57211.source: . Having a firm grasp of this formula will help your understanding in many areas.
439e25d57211.target: 。牢固掌握这个公式将有助于你在许多领域的理解。

d73b02aecadc.source: 3) Most importantly, this example shows there are many ways to understand a formula. Maybe you like the pairing method, maybe you prefer the rectangle technique, or maybe there’s another explanation that works for you.
d73b02aecadc.target: 3) 最重要的是，这个例子表明理解一个公式有多种方式。也许你喜欢配对法，也许你更喜欢矩形技巧，或者可能有其他适合你的解释。

78da654a966a.source: when you don’t understand — try to find another explanation that works. Happy math.
78da654a966a.target: 当你无法理解时——试着寻找另一个有效的解释。数学快乐。

cc279412296c.source: about the history of this story and the technique Gauss may have used.
cc279412296c.target: 关于这个故事的历史以及高斯可能使用的技巧。

68f0bf1a8c70.source: Start with the regular formula (1 + 2 + 3 + … + n = n * (n + 1) / 2) and subtract off the part you don’t want (1 + 2 + 3 + 4 = 4 * (4 + 1) / 2 = 10).

6897509674a9.source: And for any starting number a:

31b26d5345a1.source: We want to get rid of every number from 1 up to a – 1.
31b26d5345a1.target: 我们想去除从 1 到 a-1 的所有数字。

23784171ccf5.source: Just double the regular formula. To add evens from 2 to 50, find 1 + 2 + 3 + 4 … + 25 and double it:
23784171ccf5.target: 只需将常规公式加倍。要计算从 2 到 50 的偶数之和，先求 1 + 2 + 3 + 4 … + 25，然后加倍：

d5fb9ddade80.source: So, to get the evens from 2 to 50 you’d do 25 * (25 + 1) = 650
d5fb9ddade80.target: 所以，要得到从 2 到 50 的偶数之和，你计算 25 * (25 + 1) = 650

f861c1be3e61.source: That’s the same as the even formula, except each number is 1 less than its counterpart (we have 1 instead of 2, 3 instead of 4, and so on). We get the next biggest even number (n + 1) and take off the extra (n + 1)/2 “-1″ items:
f861c1be3e61.target: 这与偶数公式相同，只是每个数字比对应的数字少 1（我们有 1 而不是 2，3 而不是 4，以此类推）。我们取下一个最大的偶数 (n + 1)，然后减去多余的 (n + 1)/2 个“-1”项：

ad47bcb01e89.source: To add 1 + 3 + 5 + … 13, get the next biggest even (n + 1 = 14) and do
ad47bcb01e89.target: 要计算 1 + 3 + 5 + … + 13，取下一个更大的偶数（n + 1 = 14）并执行

e272734208fc.source: Let’s say you want the evens from 50 + 52 + 54 + 56 + … 100. Find all the evens
e272734208fc.target: 假设你想要 50 + 52 + 54 + 56 + … + 100 中的偶数。找出所有偶数

5361702b5d9f.source: and subtract off the ones you don’t want
5361702b5d9f.target: 然后减去你不想要的部分

2b61d5e7e17b.target: Ruby 极客：你可以用以下代码验证

822bd8869dea.target: 通用唯一标识符(UUID) URN命名空间

93c40b5ade73.source: GUIDs give you a unique serial number that can be used on any item in the universe.
93c40b5ade73.target: GUID(全局唯一标识符)为您提供一个可用于宇宙中任何物品的唯一序列号。

b078063bbeb3.source: personal copy of the internet, every second, for a billion years
b078063bbeb3.target: 每秒钟获取整个互联网的个人副本，持续十亿年

c4a15a88ad8a.source: Content-based (MD5 or SHA-1 hash of data):
c4a15a88ad8a.target: 基于内容（数据的MD5或SHA-1哈希值）：

4812319cf97d.source: Who does the counting? A central authority?
4812319cf97d.target: 谁来计数？一个中央权威？

5eae2f0e9163.source: Who handles simultaneous requests and eliminates duplicates?
5eae2f0e9163.target: 谁处理并发请求并消除重复？

a1caf9492094.source: Can IDs be shared between products? Is Social Security Number 1 different from ISBN 1?
a1caf9492094.target: ID可以在不同产品间共享吗？社保号1是否与ISBN 1不同？

25fa22060cb1.source: Can people guess what the next ID will be? How many IDs have been issued?
25fa22060cb1.target: 人们能猜到下一个ID是什么吗？已经发放了多少个ID？

36fb04846cdd.source: for Ruby on Rails: Use a GUID instead of an integer as a primary key in your database.
36fb04846cdd.target: 用于Ruby on Rails：在数据库中使用GUID代替整数作为主键。

1f00c9a5baab.source: Just use the system’s random-number generator to create a 128-bit number.
1f00c9a5baab.target: 只需使用系统的随机数生成器创建一个128位的数字。

aa02acad43f2.source: Create a GUID based on the current time.
aa02acad43f2.target: 基于当前时间创建一个GUID。

69aa908367bc.source: Make a GUID with certain portions based on hardware features, such as the MAC address of a network card. This isn’t great because the GUID isn’t “anonymous” and can be partially traced to the machine that created it.
69aa908367bc.target: 创建一个GUID，其中某些部分基于硬件特性，例如网卡的MAC地址。这并不好，因为GUID不是“匿名的”，并且可以部分追溯到生成它的机器。

267aad7b095e.source: Create a GUID based on a hash of the file contents. Files with the same contents will get the same GUID. You can also seed the hash with a unique namespace (like your URL).
267aad7b095e.target: 基于文件内容的哈希创建一个GUID。内容相同的文件将获得相同的GUID。你还可以使用唯一的命名空间（如你的URL）作为哈希的种子。

0967738d4deb.source: Unique primary key in databases. This lets database items created on separate machines be merged later without conflict, and without the need for a central server to manage IDs.
0967738d4deb.target: 数据库中的唯一主键。这使得在不同机器上创建的数据库项可以稍后合并而不发生冲突，且无需中央服务器来管理ID。

df0ad682be77.source: Unique filename for uploaded files. If each version of the file gets its own GUID, you can set a
df0ad682be77.target: 上传文件的唯一文件名。如果文件的每个版本都有其自己的GUID，你可以设置

69228730d90d.source: Unique name for resources (del.icio.us URL for instacalc:
69228730d90d.target: 资源的唯一名称（例如instacalc的del.icio.us URL）：

45ac1958f48c.source: Allow vendors to create and register unique IDs without contacting a central authority (like
45ac1958f48c.target: 允许供应商在不联系中央权威机构的情况下创建和注册唯一ID（例如

2bedb90fb592.source: You avoid the need for management, but can’t keep track of what’s been assigned. A compromise is to generate GUIDs internally and then hand them out.
2bedb90fb592.target: 你避免了管理的需要，但无法跟踪已分配的内容。一种折衷方案是内部生成GUID，然后分发。

67ca36388757.source: You can merge sets of GUIDs from different data sources with a microscopic chance of conflict.
67ca36388757.target: 你可以合并来自不同数据源的GUID集合，冲突概率微乎其微。

9d8437f26fd4.source: Users cannot easily guess the ID for an object they don’t know. This is good for security, difficult for debugging.
9d8437f26fd4.target: 用户很难猜出未知对象的ID。这对安全有好处，但对调试不利。

b0e3b17b84f6.source: GUIDs are an example of the time-space tradeoff. You save time in merging but have to use space to store the large (16-byte) GUID. It may not make sense to have a 16-byte GUID keeping track of a 4-byte item in your database.
b0e3b17b84f6.target: GUID是时空权衡的一个例子。你在合并时节省了时间，但需要使用空间来存储较大的（16字节）GUID。用一个16字节的GUID来跟踪数据库中一个4字节的项可能没有意义。

0312b8ee4822.source: Our world is numbered. Books have ISBNs and products have barcodes. Cars have
0312b8ee4822.target: 我们的世界被编号了。书籍有ISBN，产品有条形码。汽车有

094d604e0e4d.source: , even people have social security numbers.

cdd4ee49ff3b.source: Numbers help us reference items unambiguously. “John Smith” may be many people, but Social Security Number 123-45-6789 refers to one person exactly.
cdd4ee49ff3b.target: 数字帮助我们明确地引用物品。“John Smith”可能对应很多人，但社会安全号码123-45-6789精确地指向一个人。

bb8c7e1ec796.source: A GUID (globally unique identifier) is a bigger, badder version of this type of ID number. You may see the term UUID tossed about (universally unique identifier), a nitpicky word for those whose numbers are unique not only within the globe, but throughout the entire universe.
bb8c7e1ec796.target: GUID（全局唯一标识符）是一种更大、更强的ID号码。你可能会看到UUID（通用唯一标识符）这个术语被随意使用，这个词有些吹毛求疵，用于那些不仅在全局，而且在整个宇宙中都唯一的数字。

9ffe66596c18.source: Any way you title it, GUIDs or UUIDs are just big, gigantic ID numbers.
9ffe66596c18.target: 无论你怎么称呼，GUID或UUID都只是巨大的ID号码。

82f532e74b49.source: “We don’t need no stinkin’ GUIDs,” you may be thinking between gulps of Top Ramen, “I’ll just use regular numbers and start counting up from 1.”
82f532e74b49.target: “我们才不需要什么讨厌的GUID，”你可能会在一大口Top Ramen的间隙想，“我就用普通数字，从1开始计数。”

b7f0d8fb8955.source: Sure, it sounds easy. Just start with ISBN #1 and add one for each new book. But problems arise:
b7f0d8fb8955.target: 当然，这听起来很简单。从ISBN #1开始，每本新书加一。但问题出现了：

2fd4acccdc07.source: The problem with counting is that we want to
2fd4acccdc07.target: 计数的问题在于我们希望

7c218be2e5f0.source: GUIDs are large, enormous numbers that are nearly guaranteed to be unique. They are usually 128 bits long and look like this
7c218be2e5f0.target: GUID是大的、巨大的数字，几乎保证唯一。它们通常是128位长，看起来像这样

486f46b96582.source: The format is a well-defined sequence of 32 hex digits grouped into chunks of 8-4-4-4-12. This gives us
486f46b96582.target: 格式是一个定义良好的32个十六进制数字序列，分组为8-4-4-4-12。这给了我们

32e2d272951b.source: If you pick a huge random number (39 digits long), it’s
32e2d272951b.target: 如果你选择一个巨大的随机数（39位长），那

aef97ba8e865.source: that someone will pick the same one.
aef97ba8e865.target: 别人选到相同数字的概率极低。

87c9e94c8afa.source: GUIDs are not tied to a product. A GUID can be used for people, cars, files, webpages, colors, anything. With regular registration numbers, you start counting at 1 and numbers can overlap. Social Security Number 123-45-6789 is different from ISBN 123456789 which is different from barcode 123456789. This isn’t an issue with GUIDs.
87c9e94c8afa.target: GUID不与特定产品绑定。GUID可以用于人、汽车、文件、网页、颜色等任何事物。对于常规的注册号码，你从1开始计数，数字可能重叠。社会安全号码123-45-6789不同于ISBN 123456789，也不同于条形码123456789。这在使用GUID时不是问题。

3af0a35bfdc2.source: It’s up to the person reading the GUID to figure out the context of the GUID. There are so many GUIDs that you can use them to number everything and not run out.
3af0a35bfdc2.target: 这取决于阅读GUID的人如何理解GUID的上下文。GUID数量极其庞大，你可以用它们来编号所有东西而不会耗尽。

8a6e979611d4.source: When learning about GUIDs, it feels like 39 measly digits aren’t enough. Won’t we run out if people get GUID-crazy, assigning them for everything from their pets to their favorite bubble gum flavor?
8a6e979611d4.target: 学习GUID时，感觉区区39位数字似乎不够。如果人们滥用GUID，给宠物到最爱的口香糖口味都分配一个，难道不会耗尽吗？

9909a7c64a8c.source: Let’s see. Think about how big the Internet is: Google has billions of web pages in its index. Let’s call it a trillion (10^12) for kicks. Think about every wikipedia article, every news item on CNN, every product in Amazon, every blog post from any author. We can assign a GUID for each of these documents.
9909a7c64a8c.target: 让我们看看。想想互联网有多大：Google的索引中有数十亿个网页。就算它有一万亿（10^12）好了。再想想维基百科的每篇文章、CNN的每条新闻、亚马逊的每个产品、任何作者的每篇博客。我们可以为这些文档中的每一个分配一个GUID。

687d21eb2e76.source: Now let’s say everyone on Earth gets their own copy of the internet, to keep track of their stuff. Even crazier, let’s say each person gets their own copy of the internet every second. How long can we go on?
687d21eb2e76.target: 现在假设地球上的每个人都拥有他们自己的一份互联网来跟踪自己的东西。甚至更疯狂，假设每个人每秒都得到一份自己的互联网副本。我们能坚持多久？

1a1397ffd996.source: Let me say that again. Each person gets a
1a1397ffd996.target: 让我再说一遍。每个人得到一份

264760e936a7.source: It’s a mind-boggling amount of items, and it’s hard to get our heads around it. Trust me, we won’t run out of GUIDs anytime soon. And if we do? We’ll start using GUIDs with more digits.
264760e936a7.target: 这是一个令人难以置信的项目数量，很难理解。相信我，我们不会很快用完GUID。如果真用完了呢？我们会开始使用更多位数的GUID。

ddbe874e3837.source: If you want to create GUIDs, try the

a34865a26a70.source: There are several ways to create GUIDs (

59782970ab42.source: describes the conventions), but you want to avoid that mess and use a library. The general types of GUIDs are:
59782970ab42.target: 描述了约定），但你应该避免混乱并使用库。GUID的一般类型是：

997b59ce43dd.source: You can mix-and-match techniques above. If you want duplicate files to have the same GUID, then use GUIDs based on the contents. If you want GUIDs to be unique, even if the contents are the same, then create them randomly or with a combination of file contents and a random number.
997b59ce43dd.target: 你可以混合使用上述技术。如果你希望重复文件具有相同的GUID，则使用基于内容的GUID。如果你希望GUID唯一，即使内容相同，则随机创建或结合文件内容和随机数创建。

c6596e6ca8b0.source: Here’s a few things you can do with GUIDs:
c6596e6ca8b0.target: 以下是一些你可以用GUID做的事情：

b9df4576ed0c.source: Like most things in life, GUIDs have benefits and drawbacks. Weigh the features to see if they make sense:
b9df4576ed0c.target: 像生活中的大多数事情一样，GUID有优点和缺点。权衡特性以确定它们是否有意义：

09be0d7e4b69.source: There’s one giant caveat for GUIDs: collisions are still possible.
09be0d7e4b69.target: 对于GUID有一个巨大的警告：仍然可能发生冲突。

1608847cbecf.source: shows us the chance of a collision as
1608847cbecf.target: 向我们展示了冲突的概率为

28f548c682ae.source: .  It’s very, very unlikely that GUIDs will collide, but as more are assigned, there are fewer left to choose from.
28f548c682ae.target: 。GUID发生冲突的可能性非常非常小，但随着分配数量的增加，可供选择的剩余数量会减少。

114f7c673a2e.source: Second, a malicious user could try hijacking GUIDs that he knows will be used (assuming the user can assign their own GUIDs), or resubmitting different content to a previous GUID (submitting file A under the hash of file B).
114f7c673a2e.target: 第二，恶意用户可能试图劫持他知道将要被使用的GUID（假设用户可以分配自己的GUID），或者将不同的内容重新提交到先前的GUID（将文件A提交到文件B的哈希下）。

1a23bb96fddf.source: If you are writing software, program defensively and detect cases where the GUID already exists. Give the user an error or even better, recover, create a new GUID on the server side and try again. GUIDs are great, but they aren’t a magic bullet.
1a23bb96fddf.target: 如果你在编写软件，要进行防御性编程，检测GUID已经存在的情况。向用户显示错误，或者更好的是，恢复，在服务器端创建一个新的GUID并重试。GUID很好，但它们不是魔法子弹。

80caf5ac9924.source: As always, we’re never done learning. Read more about GUIDs here:
80caf5ac9924.target: 一如既往，我们学无止境。在这里了解更多关于GUID的信息：

7c0b4dc18fea.source: see the rule of 72 for any factor

dfb7e68de0c7.source: At 6% interest, your money takes 72/6 or 12 years to double.
dfb7e68de0c7.target: 在6%的利率下，你的资金需要72/6即12年才能翻倍。

03aeadd96dfb.source: To double your money in 10 years, get an interest rate of 72/10 or 7.2%.
03aeadd96dfb.target: 要在10年内使资金翻倍，需要获得72/10即7.2%的利率。

c5c3c2c3dbf4.source: If your country’s GDP grows at 3% a year, the economy doubles in 72/3 or 24 years.
c5c3c2c3dbf4.target: 如果你的国家GDP每年增长3%，经济将在72/3即24年内翻倍。

39e8b33ecbf2.source: If your growth slips to 2%, it will double in 36 years. If growth increases to 4%, the economy doubles in 18 years. Given the speed at which technology develops, shaving years off your growth time could be very important.
39e8b33ecbf2.target: 如果增长率降至2%，经济将在36年内翻倍。如果增长率升至4%，经济则在18年内翻倍。鉴于技术发展的速度，缩短增长时间可能非常重要。

42aead6fbb02.source: If inflation rates go from 2% to 3%, your money will lose half its value in 24 years instead of 36.
42aead6fbb02.target: 如果通胀率从2%上升到3%，你的资金将在24年内失去一半价值，而不是36年。

0a7a21c52ad2.source: If college tuition increases at 5% per year (which is faster than inflation), tuition costs will double in 72/5 or about 14.4 years. If you pay 15% interest on your credit cards, the amount you owe will
0a7a21c52ad2.target: 如果大学学费每年增长5%（比通胀快），学费将在72/5即约14.4年内翻倍。如果你为信用卡支付15%的利息，你所欠的金额将在

4d50651dc39f.source: The Rule of 72 is a great

d78492d5a9a2.source: to estimate the effect of any growth rate, from quick financial calculations to population estimates. Here’s the formula:
d78492d5a9a2.target: 用来估算任何增长率的影响，从快速财务计算到人口估计。公式如下：

773ca73b4b7d.source: and understanding the nature of compound interest. Examples:
773ca73b4b7d.target: 并理解复利的本质。示例：

b7d0f9dd7266.source: You can also use the rule of 72 for

c67acbf3d76c.source: The rule of 72 shows why a “small” 1% difference in inflation or GDP expansion has a huge effect in forecasting models.
c67acbf3d76c.target: 72法则显示了为什么通胀或GDP扩张中“微小”的1%差异会在预测模型中产生巨大影响。

b10d7b378425.source: By the way, the Rule of 72 applies to anything that grows, including population. Can you see why a population growth rate of 3% vs 2% could be a huge problem for planning? Instead of needing to double your capacity in 36 years, you only have 24. Twelve years were shaved off your schedule with one percentage point.
b10d7b378425.target: 顺便说一下，72法则适用于任何增长的事物，包括人口。你能看出为什么3%与2%的人口增长率会给规划带来巨大问题吗？原本需要36年翻倍的能力，现在只有24年。一个百分点就让你从时间表中削掉了12年。

fc5db393e621.source: Half the fun in using this magic formula is seeing how it’s made. Our goal is to figure out how long it takes for some money (or something else) to double at a certain interest rate.
fc5db393e621.target: 使用这个神奇公式的一半乐趣在于了解它是如何推导的。我们的目标是弄清楚在一定利率下，一笔钱（或别的东西）翻倍需要多长时间。

ef463898cacb.source: Let’s start with \$1 since it’s easy to work with (the exact value doesn’t matter). So, suppose we have \$1 and a yearly interest rate R. After one year we have:
ef463898cacb.target: 我们先从1美元开始，因为容易计算（具体值不重要）。假设我们有1美元，年利率为R。一年后我们有：

308904bf7849.source: For example, at 10% interest, we’d have \$1 * (1 + 0.1) = \$1.10 at the end of the year. After 2 years, we’d have
308904bf7849.target: 例如，在10%的利率下，年末我们有1美元 * (1 + 0.1) = 1.10美元。两年后，我们有

a90ea7da0f44.source: = \$1.21 at the end of year 2. Notice how the dime we earned the first year starts earning money on its own (a penny). Next year we create another dime that starts making pennies for us, along with the small amount the first penny contributes. As Ben Franklin said: “The money that money earns, earns money”, or “The dime the dollar earned, earns a penny.” Cool, huh?
a90ea7da0f44.target: = 第二年年底的1.21美元。注意第一年赚到的一角钱开始自己赚钱（一分钱）。第二年我们又创造了一角钱，开始为我们赚取一分钱，同时第一分钱也贡献了一点。正如本杰明·富兰克林所说：‘钱赚的钱，再赚钱’，或者‘美元赚到的一角钱，再赚一分钱’。很酷，对吧？

701159690636.source: This deceptively small, cumulative growth makes compound interest extremely powerful – Einstein called it one of the most powerful forces in the universe.
701159690636.target: 这种看似微小却不断累积的增长使复利变得极其强大——爱因斯坦称其为宇宙中最强大的力量之一。

ca203324d09c.target: 现在，我们需要找出翻倍所需的时间——即达到2美元。方程变为：

2d12c9b44431.source: Basically: How many years at R% interest does it take to get to 2? Not too hard, right? Let’s get to work on this sucka and find N:
2d12c9b44431.target: 基本问题：在R%的利率下，需要多少年才能达到2？不难吧？让我们来解决这个家伙，求出N：

83553f7a1394.source: There’s a little trickery on line 5. We use an approximation to say that ln(1+R) = R. It’s pretty close – even at R = .25 the approximation is 10% accurate (
83553f7a1394.target: 在第5行有一点小技巧。我们使用一个近似：ln(1+R) = R。这个近似相当接近——即使当R=0.25时，近似值的误差也在10%以内（

71710d81153c.target: ）。随着你使用更大的利率，精度会变差。

d364cd7da571.source: Now let’s clean up the formula a bit. We want to use R as an integer (3) rather than a decimal (.03), so we multiply the right hand side by 100:
d364cd7da571.target: 现在让我们稍微整理一下公式。我们希望将R用作整数（3）而不是小数（0.03），所以我们将右边乘以100：

d077b9a2be9c.source: There’s one last step: 69.3 is nice and all, but not easily divisible. 72 is closeby, and has many more factors (2, 3, 4, 6, 12…). So the rule of 72 it is. Sorry 69.3, we hardly knew ye. (We could use 70, but again, 72 is nearby and even more divisible; for a mental shortcut, go with the number easiest to divide.)
d077b9a2be9c.target: 还有最后一步：69.3虽然好，但不容易被整除。72接近，并且有更多的因数（2、3、4、6、12……）。所以就用72法则了。抱歉，69.3，我们几乎不认识你。（我们可以用70，但同样，72更近且更易整除；对于心算捷径，选择最容易除的数字。）

250375509d6a.source: This series expansion is the Calculus Way of showing how far the initial estimate strays from the actual result. The first correction term $\frac{1}{2} r \log(2)$ is small but grows with r. 72 is on the “right side” because it helps us stay in the accurate zone for longer. Neat insight!
250375509d6a.target: 这个级数展开是微积分方式展示初始估计与实际结果之间偏差的大小。第一个修正项 $\frac{1}{2} r \log(2)$ 很小，但随着r增长而增大。72位于“右侧”，因为它帮助我们更长时间地停留在精确区域内。巧妙的见解！
